preparation_method: sautéed in butter and black pepper sauce

serving.description: 1 whole crab (~500g whole weight, approximately 200–250g edible meat and sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is one whole crab of approximately 500 grams total weight, yielding roughly 200–250 grams of edible crab meat and sauce after shell removal.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, cooked with sauce — nutrient composition estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD crab entries and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180: Crustaceans, crab, blue, cooked, moist heat) adjusted for butter and black pepper sauce preparation
nutrient_source.organization: IMR Malaysia MyFCD; USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for black pepper crab dish exists in IMR MyFCD or USDA. Values estimated by combining IMR MyFCD cooked crab baseline (protein ~18g/100g, fat ~1.5g/100g) with added butter (~5g fat contribution per 100g edible portion) and oyster sauce (~2g carbohydrate contribution per 100g edible portion). Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors. Sodium elevated due to oyster sauce and seasoning. Values represent the edible portion including sauce.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of seafood and protein-dominant dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked shellfish/crab; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black pepper crab dish. GI estimated at 15 based on the low-GI profile of cooked shellfish and protein-dominant seafood dishes with minimal carbohydrate content (~3.2g/100g from oyster sauce and cornstarch). Analogous to cooked crab and similar protein-fat dominant dishes. Glycemic load per 500g serving calculated as: (15 × 16g carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.4. Carbohydrate content of edible portion (~200g) used: 200g × 3.2g/100g = 6.4g net carbs; GL = (15 × 6.4) / 100 = 0.96, rounded to 1.0 for whole serving. Revised: using 500g total serving weight for GL: 500g × 3.2/100 = 16g carbs; GL = (15 × 16)/100 = 2.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from oyster sauce and seasoning (~520mg/100g; ~2600mg per 500g whole crab serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid black pepper crab due to its very high sodium load from oyster sauce and seasoning, which can significantly elevate blood pressure; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited and paired with low-sodium foods throughout the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~3.2g/100g) and low glycemic index (~15) with high protein content supporting satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black pepper crab is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index; however, the butter and oyster sauce contribute saturated fat and sodium, so portion control and frequency of consumption should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from butter (~3.9g/100g), high sodium, and dietary cholesterol (~85mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume black pepper crab in moderation due to its saturated fat content from butter and high sodium from oyster sauce, both of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; the omega-3 fatty acids naturally present in crab provide some cardioprotective benefit but do not offset the high sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Crab is a signature seafood dish of Singapore and Malaysia, widely regarded as one of the two iconic crab preparations of the region alongside Chilli Crab. The dish features a whole mud crab (typically 500–800g) stir-fried in a wok over high heat with a fragrant, pungent sauce made from freshly cracked or coarsely ground black pepper — often Sarawak black pepper from Malaysian Borneo, prized for its bold, aromatic heat — combined with butter, garlic, ginger, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. The butter creates a rich, glossy coating that clings to the crab shell and meat, while the black pepper delivers a sharp, spicy warmth that distinguishes this dish from milder crab preparations. The crab meat is firm, sweet, and slightly briny, contrasting with the bold, savoury-spicy sauce. Black Pepper Crab is a staple of Tze Char (煮炒) restaurants — informal Chinese-style cook-and-fry eateries common in Singapore and Malaysia — and is typically served as a shared centrepiece dish with steamed white rice or mantou (fried buns) to soak up the sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~16.5g/100g) from the crab meat, moderate in fat (~7.8g/100g) primarily from butter, and very low in carbohydrates (~3.2g/100g), giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 15. Sodium content is elevated (~520mg/100g) due to oyster sauce and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, zinc, selenium, and phosphorus from the crab. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with shellfish, dairy, soy, or gluten allergies.
common_user_queries: How is black pepper crab made?
How many calories in black pepper crab?
Is black pepper crab good for diabetics?

Can I eat black pepper crab if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for black pepper crab dish exists in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by combining cooked crab baseline data from IMR MyFCD and USDA with adjustments for butter and oyster sauce contributions. GI is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein profile of cooked shellfish. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, the component sources are approved, and the macronutrient profile is consistent with the known composition of the dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the edible portion of the dish including sauce, calculated per 100g of edible yield. The whole crab serving (~500g) yields approximately 200–250g of edible meat and sauce after shell removal. 2. Sodium is elevated due to oyster sauce and seasoning; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases used for this estimation. 4. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from butter and crab fat composition data in USDA SR Legacy; these are -1 sentinel values replaced with estimates where defensible component data exists. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for black pepper crab dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 6. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains butter (dairy) and the halal certification of the specific restaurant preparation is unknown; the dish may be prepared halal at certified establishments. 7. The input record noted Sarawak black pepper and chef-made oyster sauce as key distinguishing ingredients; these have been incorporated into the nutrient estimation and description. 8. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1g/100g from butter content; this is a trace amount consistent with natural dairy trans fats (vaccenic acid) rather than industrial trans fats.
